pick.rationale: Kansas enters as a legitimate top-25 team ranked 21st in KenPom and 22nd in Torvik with an elite defense ranking 10th nationally in adjusted defensive efficiency, while California Baptist sits outside the major analytics rankings entirely with an SRS of 130th—a gap of over 100 spots that reflects the massive difference between Big 12 and WAC competition. The Lancers' first-ever NCAA tournament appearance is a nice story, and their 32nd-ranked defense could keep the game competitive for stretches, but their 237th-ranked offense will struggle mightily against Kansas's elite defense. Star freshman Darryn Peterson, a potential #1 NBA draft pick, has been cleared for the tournament after battling injuries and played 37 minutes in the Big 12 tournament, giving the Jayhawks a go-to scorer that CBU cannot match. The 12.5-14.5 point spread reflects this substantial talent gap, and Bill Self's tournament experience against a program making its March Madness debut makes this one of the safer 4/13 matchups on the board.

Jordan Guskey Topeka Capital-Journal Show Caption Hide Caption Darryn Peterson reflects on difficulties of Kansas basketball season Check out some of what Kansas basketball freshman Darryn Peterson had to say Friday after the Jayhawks suffered a Big 12 tournament loss vs Houston. LAWRENCE — Darryn Peterson is viewed as someone who could carry a team, for a matter of weeks, in postseason play. Peterson, a star freshman for Kansas basketball, has the talent to be the No. 1 pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. That he’s still in that conversation, despite missing as much time as he has this season due to various health issues, reinforces that potential. Should the Jayhawks go on a run in this year’s men’s NCAA tournament, starting out as the No. 4 seed in the East Region with a game Friday against No. 13 seed California Baptist in San Diego, California, odds are Peterson is leading the way. But while Kansas (23-10) had the chance to see an opener of sorts, of Peterson in postseason play with the Big 12 Conference tournament in Kansas City, Missouri, that doesn’t mean what was on display is the ceiling of what’s possible. For KU coach Bill Self, in fact, it clearly wasn’t. That level of expectation surrounding Peterson, seems to be something everyone is still chasing. Kansas basketball March Madness tickets “He’ll be excited,” Self said about Peterson looking ahead to the NCAA tournament. “I didn’t see what I — none of us played well enough to see what we could do in Kansas City. So, it’ll be interesting to see how everybody plays. So many people put so much emphasis on him, which I guess is fair with the hype, but the reality is I want him to make us better and I want us to make him better. And I didn’t think we maxed out or came even close to that as what it could be in Kansas City.” Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY’s Survivor Pool today! Peterson finished with 24 points and eight rebounds in the Big 12 tournament quarterfinals win against TCU, and 14 points and four rebounds in the semifinal loss against Houston. Neither game saw him shoot the ball well at all from the field, at least certainly not to the extent he’s capable of, although he certainly delivered each night at the free throw line. For the season now, across his 22 games with 21 starts, he’s averaging 19.8 points and 4.4 rebounds per game. Start to generate more momentum with Friday’s round of 64 game in the NCAA tournament against California Baptist, and Peterson and the Jayhawks can put themselves in a position to take a shot at reaching another Sweet 16 for the program. The game is set to tip off at 8:45 p.m. (CT) in San Diego, California. March Madness will be in full swing. Jordan Guskey covers University of Kansas Athletics at The Topeka Capita

LAWRENCE — Darryn Peterson is viewed as someone who could carry a team, for a matter of weeks, in postseason play. Peterson, a star freshman for Kansas basketball, has the talent to be the No. 1 pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. That he’s still in that conversation, despite missing as much time as he has this season due to various health issues, reinforces that potential. Should the Jayhawks go on a run in this year’s men’s NCAA tournament, starting out as the No. 4 seed in the East Region with a game Friday against No. 13 seed California Baptist in San Diego, California, odds are Peterson is leading the way. But while Kansas (23-10) had the chance to see an opener of sorts, of Peterson in postseason play with the Big 12 Conference tournament in Kansas City, Missouri, that doesn’t mean what was on display is the ceiling of what’s possible. For KU coach Bill Self, in fact, it clearly wasn’t. That level of expectation surrounding Peterson, seems to be something everyone is still chasing. Kansas basketball March Madness tickets “He’ll be excited,” Self said about Peterson looking ahead to the NCAA tournament. “I didn’t see what I — none of us played well enough to see what we could do in Kansas City. So, it’ll be interesting to see how everybody plays. So many people put so much emphasis on him, which I guess is fair with the hype, but the reality is I want him to make us better and I want us to make him better. And I didn’t think we maxed out or came even close to that as what it could be in Kansas City.” Ready to win March Madness? Join USA TODAY’s Survivor Pool today! Peterson finished with 24 points and eight rebounds in the Big 12 tournament quarterfinals win against TCU, and 14 points and four rebounds in the semifinal loss against Houston. Neither game saw him shoot the ball well at all from the field, at least certainly not to the extent he’s capable of, although he certainly delivered each night at the free throw line. For the season now, across his 22 games with 21 starts, he’s averaging 19.8 points and 4.4 rebounds per game. Start to generate more momentum with Friday’s round of 64 game in the NCAA tournament against California Baptist, and Peterson and the Jayhawks can put themselves in a position to take a shot at reaching another Sweet 16 for the program. The game is set to tip off at 8:45 p.m. (CT) in San Diego, California. March Madness will be in full swing. Jordan Guskey covers University of Kansas Athletics at The Topeka Capita
